edge: AbstractVideo anomaly detection (VAD) is described as a significant computer vision problem.
Existing VAD methods are based on deep neural networks (DNN).
Existing VAD methods often follow the route of frame reconstruction or frame prediction.

node_2: visual features and temporal context relationships
edge: DNN methods have limitations in mining higher-level visual features and temporal context relationships.
The new VAD paradigm is inspired by video codec theory.
A new task of video event restoration is proposed based on keyframes, aiming to motivate DNN to infer missing frames.

node_2: u-shaped swin transformer network with dual skip connections (ustn-dsc)
edge: USTN-DSC is proposed for video event restoration to assist in restoring both static and dynamic motion object features.
A simple and effective loss is proposed to constrain the motion consistency of the video sequence during event restoration.
VAD is a research topic in computer vision.
Challenging aspect due to scarcity of anomalous samples.
Scarcity of anomalous samples makes it hard for supervised methods to show abilities.
Unsupervised methods are more explored direction in VAD.
Reconstruction and prediction-based approaches are representative paradigms in VAD.
Reconstruction and prediction-based approaches are representative paradigms in VAD.
DAE is used in reconstruction-based approaches for learning in VAD.
DNN is an acronym for deep neural networks.
Reconstruction-based approaches use deep autoencoder (DAE) to learn and reconstruct input frames.
DAE enables well reconstruction of anomalous frames.
DAE memorizes low-level details rather than understanding high-level semantics.
Prediction-based approach assumes that anomalous events are unpredictable.
Model predicts future frames based on past frames, and poor predictions indicate anomalies.
Short-term temporal relationships are considered to model appearance and motion patterns.
Inspired by video codec theory, a brand-new VAD paradigm based on video event restoration is proposed.
VAD paradigm focuses on video event restoration.
The VAD paradigm proposed is inspired by the video codec theory.
The video codec theory introduces keyframes as an important concept.
Keyframes are related to the three types of frames - I-frame, P-frame, and B-frame.
I-frame contains the complete appearance information of the current frame.

node_2: motion difference information from the previous frame
edge: P-frame contains the motion difference information from the previous frame.

node_2: motion difference between previous and next frames
edge: B-frame contains the motion difference between the previous and next frames.
Keyframes are utilized for video event restoration.
DNN is motivated to explore and learn spatio-temporal evolutionary relationships in the video for inferring missing frames.

Keyframes are essential for video event restoration as mentioned in the context. Video restoration quality is highly dependent on the regularity and temporal correlation of events in the video, making keyframes crucial for this task.
Anomalous events, which are irregular and random, result in poor video restoration. This indicates a negative correlation between video restoration quality and the nature of events being restored.
The keyframes-based video event restoration task is applicable to VAD, creating a direct relationship between these two concepts.
A comparison between video codec and video event restoration task is provided in the context, suggesting a potential relationship or contrast between these two concepts.

The proposed U-shaped Swin Transformer Network with Dual Skip Connections (USTN-DSC) is specifically designed for video event restoration based on keyframes, highlighting a specialized connection between these two concepts.

The introduction of dual skip connections in USTN-DSC aims to cope with complex motion patterns in the video for better video event restoration, emphasizing the importance of addressing these patterns in the process.

Ensuring the restored video sequence maintains consistency in temporal variation with the real video sequence is crucial for video event restoration, indicating a direct relationship between these two concepts.

The video anomaly detection paradigm is introduced to restore the video event based on keyframes, allowing for more effective mining and learning of higher-level visual features and comprehensive temporal context relationships in the video.

The USTN-DSC model introduces cross-attention and a temporal upsampling residual skip connection to assist in restoring complex dynamic and static motion object features in the video for video event restoration.
The AFD loss is proposed to constrain the motion consistency of the video sequence, ensuring a simple and effective way to maintain the consistency of motion throughout the sequence.
USTN-DSC outperforms most existing methods on three benchmarks, as demonstrated by extensive ablation experiments proving the effectiveness of USTN-DSC in comparison to other methods.

Traditional VAD methods rely on statistical models based on hand-extracted features or classical machine learning techniques, as exemplified by the work of Adam et al. in characterizing the normal behaviors.
Features are extracted using classical machine learning techniques.
Adam et al. characterized the normal local histograms of optical based on statistical monitoring of low-level observations at multiple spatial locations.
Kim and Grauman modeled the local optical flow pattern with a mixture of probabilistic principal component analyzers and trained a space-time markov random field to infer abnormalities.
Cong et al. introduced a sparse reconstruction cost over the normal dictionary to measure the normality of testing samples.
Anomaly detection methods based on deep learning techniques.
Hasan et al. used the extracted features as input to a fully connected neural network-based autoencoder to learn the temporal regularity in the video.
A regulartiy score was calculated according to the reconstruction error and used to determine whether an abnormality occurs.
Xu et al. proposed a stacked denoising autoencoder to separately learn both the appearance and the motion features.

Xu et al. proposed to separately learn both the appearance and the motion features
Liu et al. presented a video anomaly detection method that predicts the future frame with the U-Net architecture
Yang et al. introduced a dynamic local aggregation network with adaptive clusterer for enhancing the representation capability of normal prototypes in the prediction paradigm
Video restoration, such as video super-resolution, denoising, deblurring, and inpainting, has become a popular research topic in recent years
Geng et al. proposed a real-time spatial temporal transformer to effectively incorporate all the spatial and temporal information for synthesizing high frame rate and high resolution videos
Kim et al. proposed a fast online video deblurring method by efficiently increasing the receptive field of the network without adding a computational overhead to handle large motion blurs
Sheth et al. proposed an unsupervised deep video denoiser, a convolutional neural network
Sheth et al. proposed an unsupervised deep video denoiser, a convolutional neural network architecture designed to be trained exclusively with noisy data.
The proposal by Sheth et al. is referenced by [39].
Zou et al. proposed a progressive temporal feature alignment network for video inpainting, which fills the missing regions by making use of both temporal convolution and optical flow.
This paper proposes a new video restoration task that restores the video event based on keyframes, differing from existing video restoration tasks.
The video restoration task in this paper is based on keyframes to restore the video event, requiring strong regularity and temporal correlation of events.
Restoring the video event based on keyframes can be well applied to Video Anomaly Detection (VAD).
Inspired by video codec theory, a novel normal behavior modeling paradigm for VAD is proposed: Restore video event based on Keyframes to detect anomalies.
Video events are stored based on keyframes to detect anomalies.

node_2: st block with shifted window-based multi-head self-attention (sw-msa)
edge: Features are processed by a ST block with shifted window-based multi-head self-attention to implement cross-window interactions.
The interaction capability allows capturing long-range dependencies in both spatial and temporal dimensions.

node_2: downsampled by a convolutional layer with a stride of two
edge: The outputs of the ST blocks are downsampled by a convolutional layer with a stride of two, serving as the input of the next encoder stage.
The decoder of USTN-DSC also consists of four stages, each followed by an ST decoder block with a deconvolution layer for upsampling.
Missing frames in the decoding phase are restored mainly by means of the conversion of features from the keyframes extracted from the encoder part.
Missing video frames may contain objects with slow or large motion patterns, which need to be synthesized by inferring the spatio-temporal relationship of the keyframes.
